problem_summary: Predict 2028 Olympic medal counts (including first-time winners) and quantify the effect of 'great coaches' on medal gains, using historical data, and derive policy insights for National Olympic Committees.
models_used: Hurdle model (two-stage: Logit classification + truncated count distribution)
key_techniques: Feature engineering (athlete number discretization, RCA index, PCA for medal dimension reduction, lagged host/medal terms)
Model complementation (Hurdle for sports powers, Tobit for weak/zero-medal countries)
Zero-inflated and right-censored data handling via Hurdle-Tobit fusion
Instrumental variable design and 2SLS to correct endogeneity in coach effect estimation
Memory-enhanced sliding window data preprocessing for Bayesian change-point detection
Model validation with MSE, R², 95% confidence interval coverage, and prediction accuracy stratified by medal count
Sensitivity analysis on binary classifier choice within Hurdle (random forest, decision tree, logistic regression)
Variable reconstruction to separate host country strategic event additions (RCA_add) from long-term competitiveness (RCA_usual)
Identification of nonlinear threshold effects via discretizing athlete counts and NAE categories
why_it_won: The paper expertly addresses the core statistical challenges of Olympic medal prediction—zero inflation, overdispersion, and heterogeneity—with a well-justified fusion of Hurdle and Tobit models. It demonstrates clear superiority over machine learning baselines through rigorous validation and extends the analysis with innovative causal inference (2SLS) and change-point detection to quantify the 'great coach effect'. The integration of all three tasks into a cohesive narrative, grounded in data-driven insights and actionable policy recommendations, demonstrates exceptional modeling depth and practical relevance.
section_highlights: The writing is exceptionally well-structured, with each section building logically from data processing to comparative modeling, validation, and policy insights. Tables and figures (RCA heatmaps, change-point plots, coefficient tables) are effectively used to communicate complex results visually. The paper excels in interpreting coefficients in economic/strategic terms (e.g., host effect attenuation, double-edged sword of sport breadth), making the analysis accessible and impactful.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Reliance on historical data may not capture emerging sports, sudden policy changes, or political bans.
Small sample of host country observations (only ~1% of data) limits the statistical power of host effect estimates.
Discretization of athlete numbers into categories sacrifices some granular information.
Coach effect identification via Bayesian change-point detection may attribute non-coach-related breakthroughs to great coaches (false positives).
Collinearity between host country variable and other predictors (NAE, RCA) may partially dilute the host effect in some models.
Assumption of normal Olympic holding conditions (no postponements or cancellations) restricts generalizability.
reusable_patterns: Adopting a two-stage Hurdle model to separately model the probability of winning any medal (binary) and the count of medals given success (truncated count), ideal for zero-inflated count data.

Applying Tobit model for left-censored outcomes (zero medals) while capturing continuous latent performance, and comparing with Hurdle to leverage model complementarity across different data segments.
Constructing a Revealed Comparative Advantage (RCA) index to quantify sport-specific dominance as a predictive feature.
Using Bayesian change-point detection on preprocessed sport-level time series to identify structural breaks indicative of intervention effects (e.g., coach changes).
Employing two-stage least squares (2SLS) with an instrument derived from historical performance indices to estimate causal impacts while mitigating endogeneity.
Discretizing continuous predictors (athlete counts, experience measures) into categories to capture nonlinear effects and handle future prediction uncertainty.
Conducting a sensitivity analysis of model components (e.g., choice of binary classifier in the first stage of Hurdle) to verify robustness.
